description:
You are the system administrator of several internal servers.  
    Every hour, a tiny heartbeat line is appended to a per-server log file located inside
    /home/user/server_logs/.  
    Your goal is to create a one-line-per-server **status summary** in CSV format that upper-management
    tools can ingest.

    1. Source data  
       • Directory to inspect: /home/user/server_logs/  
       • File pattern: "*.log" (for example: "web01.log", "db01.log", …).  
       • Each log line is already in the form  
         YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS status=UP  
         or  
         YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS status=DOWN  

    2. Transformation rules  
       a. For every *.log file, count how many lines contain the literal string "status=UP".  
       b. In the same file, count how many lines contain the literal string "status=DOWN".  
       c. Strip the ".log" extension from the file name; that becomes the “server” field.

    3. Output file  
       • Create the new file /home/user/server_logs/status_summary.csv  
       • Exact CSV layout (comma-separated, **no** extra spaces):  
           server,total_up,total_down  
           <server-name-1>,<UP-count-1>,<DOWN-count-1>  
           <server-name-2>,<UP-count-2>,<DOWN-count-2>  
           …  
       • Order the data lines alphabetically by the server field.  
       • End the file with a single trailing newline (LF).  
       • Nothing else should be printed to the file.

    4. Verification hint  
       The automated tester will:  
       • Read /home/user/server_logs/status_summary.csv  
       • Confirm the header line is exactly as specified.  
       • Confirm that each server present in the original *.log files appears exactly once, that counts are correct, and that alphabetical order is respected.

    5. Practical constraints  
       • You do not have root privileges; stay inside /home/user.  
       • Achieve the task using standard GNU/Linux command-line tools in as few steps as you find reasonable (usually 3-4 commands suffice).  
       • Do not edit the original log files; only read them and write the new CSV file.

    When finished, you should be able to run:
       cat /home/user/server_logs/status_summary.csv
    and see the correctly formatted summary.
truth:
Initial filesystem state (before agent starts):
    1. Directory /home/user/server_logs/ exists with default user write permissions (0755).
    2. Files and their exact contents (each line terminated by LF):

    File: /home/user/server_logs/web01.log
    ----------------------------------------
    2023-06-01 00:00:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 01:00:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 02:00:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 03:00:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 04:00:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 05:00:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 06:00:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 07:00:01 status=DOWN
    2023-06-01 08:00:01 status=DOWN
    2023-06-01 09:00:01 status=DOWN

    File: /home/user/server_logs/db01.log
    -------------------------------------
    2023-06-01 00:30:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 01:30:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 02:30:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 03:30:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 04:30:01 status=UP
    2023-06-01 05:30:01 status=DOWN
    2023-06-01 06:30:01 status=DOWN
    2023-06-01 07:30:01 status=DOWN
    2023-06-01 08:30:01 status=DOWN
    2023-06-01 09:30:01 status=DOWN

    Expected file after the agent completes the task:

    File: /home/user/server_logs/status_summary.csv
    ------------------------------------------------
    server,total_up,total_down
    db01,5,5
    web01,7,3

    (A single LF follows the last line.)
